text: Laparoscopic partial nephrectomy in a patient with toxic fat and no intraoperative ultrasound. Nephron sparing surgery is the treatment of choice in renal tumors less than four centimeters. In certain cases, toxic perirenal fat is encountered which is thick and adherent to the renal capsule. Identification of the tumor is difficult without intraoperative ultrasound. This is a case of a sixty-four years old male who consulted due to an incidental finding of three point three by two point nine by three point seven centimeters exophytic enhancing mass at the inferior pole of his left kidney upon executive checkup.

The surgeon performs a partial nephrectomy, beginning with the dissection and mobilization of the colon. The gonadal vein and ureter are identified, followed by dissection towards the renal hilum. The renal artery is isolated during the procedure.

The surgeon opens the Gerota's fascia to expose the renal mass. Due to the presence of thick, adherent toxic perirenal fat, an additional port is added for better access. Dissection and adhesiolysis are performed to separate the toxic fat from the kidney.

The surgeon visualizes the renal mass surrounded by adherent perirenal fat. After dissecting the toxic perirenal fat, the renal mass attached to the kidney is clearly visible. The margins of resection are marked, and a laparoscopic bulldog clamp is applied to the renal artery before proceeding with the tumor resection.

Following the tumor resection, the surgeon performs the first layer repair closure of the collecting system, followed by the second layer repair closure of the renal capsule. The renal artery clamp is then removed, and the Gerota's fascia is repaired.
The surgeon checks for bleeders and ensures hemostasis before placing a Jackson-Pratt (JP) drain. A laparoscopic retrieval bag is then introduced to collect the excised specimen.

Total operative time was three hours and twenty-two minutes, warm ischemia time of nineteen minutes, and with minimal blood loss, the patient was discharged on the third postoperative day. Pathology report revealed, renal cell carcinoma, clear cell type, tumor size of three centimeters in largest dimension, WHO, ISUP nuclear grade one. No definite lymphovascular invasion observed. Margins of resection negative for tumor. This is the actual specimen postoperatively.

Laparoscopic partial nephrectomy can be done without intraoperative ultrasound, in select cases, with toxic perirenal fat. Careful correlation of CT scan images and intraoperative anatomy is a must. Finding an area of normal kidney first is key to avoid disrupting the tumor or the renal capsule.
